coredata.dc:title: Full-color room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield via conjugation regulation

coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have demonstrated significant application potential in various fields such as optoelectronic devices, information encryption, and bioimaging due to their superior optical properties. However, the realization of multicolor-tunable RTP materials with both long afterglow lifetime and high quantum yield simultaneously remains a great challenging. Herein, precursor molecules with various degrees of conjugation and different energy gaps were selected and combined with ammonium borate through a one-step pyrolysis method to successfully fabricate nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) based RTP materials with a long afterglow lifetime (880 ms) and ultrahigh phosphorescence quantum yield (81.13 %) simultaneously. As the conjugation degree of the precursors increases, the phosphorescent color tuning of the composites exhibits a satisfactory transition from blue to red. Leveraging these superior properties, the resultant N-CDs-based RTP materials have been applied to the fields of advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides an important pathway for developing RTP materials with tunable color, but also proposes an engineering strategy for achieving tunable bandgaps in carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescent materials
Carbon dots
Multicolor-tunable
Conjugation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by from the Key Realm R&D Project of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2021B0707010003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12174119 , 52102042 , 52172142 ), the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou (Grant No. 202103000059 ), and the Science and Technology Program of Maoming, China (Grant No. 2021S0088 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs-I, (b) TEM image of N-CDs-II, (c) TEM image of N-CDs-III, and (d) TEM image of N-CDs-IV; the inset in the upper left corner shows the particle size distribution, and the inset in the lower right corner is the high-resolution TEM image. (e) Normalized afterglow emission spectra of N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV under 365 nm excitation. (f) Photographs of N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV under a 365 nm UV lamp when turned on and off. (g) CIE chromaticity diagram of the afterglow emission for N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs- III, and N-CDs-IV.

(a) X-ray diffraction patterns of the four types of N-CDs and the blank control sample CK. (b) Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of the four types of N-CDs and the blank control sample CK. (c-e) High-resolution X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the B 1s, O 1s, and N 1s regions for N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV.

(a) Afterglow images of the four types of N-CDs after the irradiation at 254 and 365 nm is turned off. (b-e) The three −dimensional phosphorescence emission pattern for N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV. (f) Phosphorescence emission peaks under optimal excitation. (g) Fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra under 365 nm excitation. (h) Phosphorescence decay curves powders at optimal excitation and emission for N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV.

(a-d) Variable-temperature spectra of the four types of N-CDs. (e) The summary of QY of some full-color RTP materials in the previously reported and N-CDs. (f) Schematic of the design strategy of the multi-confined phosphorescence and schematic of the protective structure of the matrix and possible intermolecular forces in N-CDs. (g) Afterglow images of the six types of N-CDs after the 365 nm irradiation is turned off. (h) Schematic diagram of the afterglow mechanism for N-CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of the anti-counterfeiting images of plum blossoms and shamrocks. (b) Schematic diagram of digital encryption and anti-counterfeiting. (c) Schematic diagram of encoding for advanced dynamic information encryption.
Schematic illustration of the design and preparation of multicolor N- CDs RTP materials.

head.ce:title: Full-color room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield via conjugation regulation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education
Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture College of Materials and Energy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have demonstrated significant application potential in various fields such as optoelectronic devices, information encryption, and bioimaging due to their superior optical properties. However, the realization of multicolor-tunable RTP materials with both long afterglow lifetime and high quantum yield simultaneously remains a great challenging. Herein, precursor molecules with various degrees of conjugation and different energy gaps were selected and combined with ammonium borate through a one-step pyrolysis method to successfully fabricate nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) based RTP materials with a long afterglow lifetime (880 ms) and ultrahigh phosphorescence quantum yield (81.13 %) simultaneously. As the conjugation degree of the precursors increases, the phosphorescent color tuning of the composites exhibits a satisfactory transition from blue to red. Leveraging these superior properties, the resultant N-CDs-based RTP materials have been applied to the fields of advanced information security and anti-counterfeiting. This work not only provides an important pathway for developing RTP materials with tunable color, but also proposes an engineering strategy for achieving tunable bandgaps in carbon materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials, known for their long-lasting luminescence after excitation [1], are valued for their extended lifetimes and larger Stokes shifts [2] compared to fluorescent materials. This leads to broad applications and significant interest in fields such as biological imaging [3,4], information encryption [5,6,7], optoelectronic devices [8], and chemical sensing [9,10]. Carbon dots (CDs) are particularly attractive for RTP applications due to their low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and unique optical properties [11]. However, they face challenges with low intersystem crossing efficiency, which hinders triplet state transitions and performance [12].

Up to now, several strategies have been developed to adjust and optimize the characteristics of RTP CDs, such as dopped with heteroatoms (N, S, P, etc.) or embedded in specific matrices [13,14,15]. When the luminescent molecules contain heteroatoms, the spin–orbit coupling effect increases the probability of electron spin flipping and intersystem crossing (ISC) within the molecules, thereby promoting the occurrence of afterglow [16]. By embedding CDs into various matrices, such as certain polymers, melamine [17], potassium alum [18], zeolites [19], silica [20], alumina [21], urea [22], polyvinyl alcohol [23] and other substances, the intermolecular interactions or hydrogen bonds between CDs and the matrix can capture CDs in a rigid environment, effectively suppressing the vibration, rotation, and collision of chromophores, and preventing the quenching of triplet excitons. In the early studies of CDs’ phosphorescence, the emission color was relatively uniform, mostly concentrated in the blue light spectrum [24]. As research has progressed, the limitation of a single phosphorescent color for material applications has become increasing apparent. Materials with tunable multicolor are more promising for practical applications. Therefore, the design and synthesis of new CDs with tunable RTP have become one of the research hotspots.

Recent advancements have seen continuous innovation in the synthesis and functional design of CDs, expanding their luminescence capabilities from single to multicolor. This progress includes exploring various preparation methods and property regulations to meet specific application needs. Strategies for achieving multicolor RTP emissions in CDs have been increasingly applied. For instance, Meng et al. [25] integrated CDs with multiple emission peaks into a MOF matrix, resulting in RTP emissions ranging from blue to red. Yang’s group [26] prepared a long-life RTP CDs-based materials with excitation-dependent full-visible light afterglow at 77 K. Wang et al. [27] created full-color CDs@PAM composites through a hydrothermal method. Another widely used method is to construct an energy transfer system [28], utilizing phosphorescent CDs as donors and fluorescent dyes as acceptors, which has also been employed to modulate afterglow emissions for multicolor effects. For instance, Cheng and colleagues [29] used this method to achieve long-lasting multicolor afterglow, shifting colors from green to orange with a lifetime over 0.9 s. Despite these achievements, the complexity in synthesizing and controlling multicolor RTP CDs still poses a challenge to their further development and practical applications.

It is known that most precursors used for the preparation of CDs originate from aromatic compounds constructed by conjugated bonds [30], which have the potential to be high luminescent efficiency materials due to their large π bonds and rigid structures [31]. CDs prepared from different precursors exhibit different structures, surface states, and luminescent properties [32], moreover, a larger conjugated size of carbon atoms will lead to a reduction in the bandgap, resulting in a redshift of the emission wavelength [33]. Therefore, it is crucial to select the appropriate precursor structure to regulate the performance of the CDs. Additionally, phosphorescence lifetime and phosphorescence quantum yield are two critical parameters for evaluating the performance of phosphorescent materials. However, there often exists a trade-off between these two parameters, such that increasing the quantum yield may reduce the phosphorescence lifetime, and vice versa. Typically, an increase in the rate of phosphorescence emission is associated with an enhancement of the phosphorescence quantum yield, concomitant with a reduction in phosphorescence lifetime. Therefore, exploring a new generation of CDs based RTP material that possesses both a long afterglow lifetime and high quantum yield simultaneously through a simple and efficient method is very desirable but challenging, which is also a key factor that needs to be solved in practical applications.

Herein, we report a novel and effective strategy for the design and preparation of multicolored CDs-based RTP materials by selecting precursors with varying degrees of conjugation. It is found that the energy gap of the CDs is influenced by the structure of the precursors. As the number of benzene rings and functional groups in the precursors increases, the optical bandgap of the resulting CDs continuously narrows, leading to a redshift of the RTP and thus exhibiting excellent afterglow performance across a range from blue to red. More importantly, the prepared N-CDs are characterized by a long afterglow life (880 ms) and an ultrahigh quantum yield (81.13 %) simultaneously. Due to their superior optical properties, the resulted CDs-based RTP materials show promising potential in advanced anti-counterfeiting measures.

To achieve efficient RTP in CDs, the preparation strategy must adhere to certain principles. Firstly, CDs should be closely combined with the matrix, with hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds formed to effectively inhibit molecular vibration and stabilize the triplet excited state of the molecule. Secondly, selecting molecules with rigid conjugated structures helps to reduce the non-radiative relaxation process [34], and tight intermolecular packing may also limit the vibration of the molecules [35]. The phosphorescent lifetime and quantum yield are optimized by the regulation between molecule and matrix. Lastly, the energy gap of the material is regulated by molecules with different structures [36] to realize the different color emission.

According to the above design concept, we developed a novel strategy for molecular structure regulation of the carbon precursor to construct efficient long-lived and high quantum yield afterglow materials. As shown in Scheme 1 , we selected ammonium borate (AB) and four different aromatic precursors as raw materials. Firstly, four precursors of benzamide, dibenzoylmethane, 1,8-naphthalimide, and 1,4,5,8-naphthalene tetracarboxylic anhydride, are rich in O or N atoms, have an n-π* transition trend, and can promote the ISC process. Secondly, these four precursors exhibit different numbers of benzene rings and functional groups. Further calculations revealed that the energy gaps between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) for the optimal structures of these four precursors are 9.095, 6.41, 4.872, and 2.627 eV, respectively (Fig. S1). The different energy gaps indicate varying degrees of conjugation among them. Moreover, the four precursors only need to be mixed with an AB solution and baked at an appropriate temperature of 180 °C for 5 h to be completed. During the heating process, the triplet excitons of the CDs are effectively stabilized by confinement action. In addition, due to the different numbers of benzene rings and conjugated structures, and the continuous red shift of the emission wavelength with the increase of the conjugated size [37], the four nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) obtained by pyrolysis at certain temperature can achieve multicolor coverage and exhibit excellent afterglow performance. Therefore, by simply adjusting the structure of the precursor, the phosphorescence of CDs RTP materials can be easily regulated. More surprisingly, the resulted N-CDs exhibit a long afterglow lifetime and ultrahigh phosphorescence quantum yield.

To investigate the morphology and structure of N-CDs, we employed transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for in-depth analysis, which provides excellent conditions for observing their morphology. The N-CDs synthesized from the four precursors are uniformly distributed in water (Fig. 1 a–d). TEM images reveal uniform spherical particles, and the particle size distribution diagrams show that the average particle sizes of N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV are 1.9, 1.9, 3.2, and 3.8 nm, respectively (insets in Fig. 1a–d). The lattice fringe spacing is approximately 0.21 nm, matching the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [38], indicating excellent crystallinity [39]. As shown in Fig. 1e, the normalized afterglow emission spectra under 365 nm excitation each exhibit the emission peaks for the four types of N-CDs. Similar to what is visible to the naked eye, these N-CDs display bright afterglow ranging from blue to red after the 365 nm UV lamp is turned off (Fig. 1f). By calculating the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE) coordinates, the coordinates for the four types of N-CDs powders are (0.1632, 0.1362), (0.1964, 0.5258), (0.4729, 0.5162), and (0.6348, 0.3523), respectively, which also confirms the four colors of the four N-CDs (Fig. 1g).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the N-CDs indicates clear crystallinity, with the CDs existing in a crystalline form along with the AB (Fig. 2 a). The corresponding XRD pattern of the N-CDs exhibits peaks similar to those of molten AB, indicating that the crystalline structure of the AB remains stable even after doping with trace guest molecules. Additionally, characteristic peaks of ammonium borate hydrate (PDF#21-0789) are observed at 16° and 32°. These results indicate that the CDs are uniformly embedded in the AB matrix. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) reveal the surface functional groups and chemical composition of the N-CDs. As shown in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b), the peaks of the four types of N-CDs at 3226 cm−1 and 3461 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The absorption peaks at 1435, 1194, and 924 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of B-O and C-N, B-O-H, and B-C, respectively [40]. The presence of C-N functional groups proves the N element doping into the CD, which favors the formation of hydrogen bonds. The FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs shows peaks that almost overlap with those of the fused AB, also indicating that the CDs are encapsulated within the AB. The chemical composition of the N-CDs was obtained by XPS peak fitting. Taking N-CDs-I as an example, the XPS spectra (Figs. S2b–e) reveal that N-CDs-I contains B, C, O, and N elements with contents of 19.77 %, 28.96 %, 45.95 %, and 5.32 %, respectively. In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2c–e), the three sub-peaks of B 1s located at 193.69, 192.68, and 190.52 eV correspond to B-O, BCO2, and B-C, respectively; the O 1s spectrum has two sub-peaks located at 534.74 and 532.81 eV, corresponding to CO and CO [41]. The N 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 404.93, 402.37, and 400.32 eV, corresponding to the amine-N, graphitic-N, and pyrrolic-N groups, respectively [42]. Additionally, the three sub-peaks of C 1s located at 288.67, 286.56, and 284.80 eV correspond to CO, CO/CN, and CC/CC, respectively (Fig. S2a). We suspect that small molecules are generated in situ as CDs through a single step of pyrolysis, which can be stably present within the matrix through covalent and hydrogen bonds.

To confirm the existence of the hydrogen bond network between AB and the N-CDs, FTIR spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs without nitrogen doping using boric acid as the matrix (named as B-CDs-I, B-CDs-II, B-CDs-III, and B-CDs-IV) were tested. As shown in the Fig. S3, the FTIR spectra of nitrogen-doped CDs, N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, N-CDs-IV, and N-CK, were compared with those without nitrogen doping. NH bonds were observed in N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, N-CDs-IV, and N-CK. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of N-CK were compared with the unreacted AB. The stretching vibration peaks and shapes of NH and OH or BO bonds can participate in hydrogen bond formation compared to the situation where no hydrogen bonds were formed. Meanwhile, the presence of amino nitrogen can also be seen in the XPS spectrum (Fig. 4e). Based on these results, we can confirm the existence of hydrogen bonds between AB and the N-CDs.

These results indicate that the AB can simultaneously provide boron and nitrogen atoms. Upon high-temperature pyrolysis, stable BC covalent bonds and NHCO covalent bonds are formed, which incorporate electron-withdrawing boron atoms into the CDs. The stable structure formed in the end effectively immobilizes the luminophore, suppressing the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons, resulting in an efficient afterglow emission.

To understand the RTP properties of the resulted materials, we first analyzed the fluorescence performance of the N-CDs. As shown in Figs. S4a–d, the fluorescence spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths demonstrate that, like many other CDs, their emission intensity and emission wavelength exhibit excitation dependence, with the fluorescence emission gradually red-shifting as the excitation wavelength red shifts. As depicted in Figs. S4e–h and S5, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the N-CDs-I aqueous solution shows an absorption peak at (∼235 nm) and a broad absorption band ranging from (280–400 nm). These are attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and the n-π* transitions of CO/CN [43]. The aqueous solutions of the other three N-CDs also exhibit distinct absorption peaks in the UV–visible spectrum within the 200–300 nm and 300–400 nm ranges, which are attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and the n-π* transitions of CO/CN. Additionally, the characteristic absorption bands observed in their excitation spectra almost completely overlap with the corresponding bands in the absorption spectra, indicating the absence of non-radiative transitions [6]. From the UV absorption spectrum, there is also a significant redshift in the peak from the high energy to low energy regions, which may be related to an increase in the conjugation of the precursor.

The result show that the four types of N-CDs exhibit excellent RTP performance under different UV lamps. As shown in Fig. 3 a, when the 254 nm UV excitation light is turned off, the N-CDs-I powder emits deep blue afterglow, and after the 365 nm excitation light is turned off, the afterglow color of N-CDs shifts to cyan. The other three types of N-CDs exhibited green, yellow and red afterglow, respectively, achieving full coverage of the visible light spectrum with afterglow colors. Therefore, their phosphorescence spectra were also tested to study the RTP properties (Fig. 3b–e, Fig. S4i–l). The phosphorescence spectrum of N-CDs-I powder primarily emits blue light. At the optimal excitation of 274 nm and the optimal emission of 419 nm, its average lifetime reaches 1770 ms, exhibiting a composite decay with a triple-exponential characteristic. The other N-CDs also exhibit strong afterglow, with the optimal afterglow emission wavelengths of N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV being 502, 558, and 609 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b–e). Phosphorescence spectrum indicates optimal emission and excitation centers for N-CDs. Under optimal excitation, corresponding phosphorescence peaks fall within wavelength ranges for blue, green, yellow, and red, aligning with visible color changes (Fig. 3f). Moreover, all four N-CDs show a shift of more than 20 nm between the FL (fluorescence) and Phos (phosphorescence) peaks under 365 nm excitation, indicating a significant Stokes shift (Fig. 3g). Despite of partial overlap of the fluorescence peaks with the phosphorescence peaks in the sample, the phosphorescence peaks are predominant, this can preliminarily be attributed to the afterglow originating from phosphorescence emission, enabling full coverage of color phosphorescence from blue to red under 365 nm excitation. Their average lifetimes can be adjusted within a range of 25 to 1770 ms (Fig. 3h). Additionally, the reactions at different temperatures (180 °C, 200 °C, and 220 °C) were carried out. The different pyrolysis temperatures have a certain impact on the phosphorescence intensity but have a minimal effect on the color of the emitted light. Thus, this work provides a new strategy and reference for the general fabrication of color-tunable ultraviolet (UV) excited CDs-based RTP materials.

Investigating the afterglow emission mechanism is of great significance for CDs based RTP materials. It was found that the four precursor powders did not detect the afterglow emission signal at room temperature before pyrolysis, indicating that the excellent properties of the four N-CDs were obtained by high temperature pyrolysis combined with AB. To further investigate the afterglow mechanism of N-CDs, variable-temperature spectroscopy was performed. As can be analyzed from Fig. 4 a–d, the phosphorescence emission intensity of N-CDs decreases gradually with increasing temperature from 77 to 350 K, due to the enhanced non-radiative transitions caused by thermal activation, which significantly reduce the emission intensity at higher temperatures. When the temperature decreases, the phosphorescence intensity increases, as the vibrational motion is effectively restricted and non-radiative transitions are suppressed at lower temperatures. These experimental results indicate that the afterglow emission of the N-CDs is essentially phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence [44,45]. As depicted in Fig. 4e and S6, the as-resulted N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV exhibit significant afterglow emission characteristic, with the absolute quantum yields (QYs) at optimal excitation of 58.16 %, 81.13 %, 62.52 %, and 8.86 %, respectively. The detail luminescence data for the four N-CDs are listed in Table S1. It is noteworthy that the phosphorescence QYs of the resultant N-CDs are significantly higher than that of most current full-color CDs reported based on RTP materials (Table S2). Interestingly, N-CDs-II not only demonstrates a high phosphorescence quantum yield of 81.13 % but also exhibits a long phosphorescence lifetime of 880 ms.

Furthermore, time-resolved femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy (fs-TA) was conducted to measure the exciton dynamics and disclose the reasons behind the ultrahigh QY of N-CDs-II of N-CDs-II. The excitation wavelength was set to 375 nm, with a probe range of 320–650 nm and a resolution of 100 fs. As shown in the Fig. S7, a distinct negative peak was observed after 0.5 ps, which is assigned to stimulated emission (SE) due to its similar peak position and shape to the fluorescence (FL) emission [46,47]. Additionally, a broad and low-intensity peak appeared in the range of 450 nm to 650 nm due to excited-state absorption (ESA). This indicates that the Stokes shift occurs on a timescale much shorter than 0.5 ps. As the decay time increased from 0.5 ps to 1000 ps, the SE exhibited a continuous shift, indicating a change in the energy state of electron-hole recombination. Finally, the SE peak could stabilize at 370–450 nm. Concurrently, the SE and ESA peaks decreased over time, indicating a stable phosphorescence emission process, and N-CDs-II exhibited typical excitation-independent behavior. The low-intensity ESA signal, compared to the strong SE, explains the high quantum yield (QY). In summary, the pump–probe shows an ultrafast Stokes shift within less than 0.5 ps, forming the emission state, followed by a clear radiative decay channel matching the steady-state emission. It can also be noted that this emission does not compete with any photo-induced absorption (PIA) [48,49]. We infer that the carbonyl groups in diphenylmethane molecules that offer effective electronic transition pathways, especially π-π* transitions, making it highly efficient and contributing to the high QY.

Further investigation was conducted to explore the phosphorescence mechanism. Under low-temperature conditions (77 K), the reverse intersystem crossing process is inhibited. The afterglow emission of N-CDs corresponds to the emission from the T1 state. By comparing the afterglow spectrum at 77 K with the fluorescence spectrum, the ΔEst values for the aforementioned materials was calculated to be 0.79, 0.53, 0.68, and 0.97 eV, respectively (Fig. S8). The energy gap is significantly larger than that required for phosphorescence emission (ΔEst > 0.3e), which is based on the process of intersystem crossing from the singlet state to the triplet state [50], thereby creating suitable conditions for phosphorescence. By calculating the optical bandgap energy at the optimal emission for the four N-CDs, the energy gaps for N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV were determined to be 2.74, 2.47, 2.22, and 2.04 eV, respectively. Moreover, the energy gaps of the four different precursors are 9.095, 6.41, 4.872, and 2.627 eV, respectively (Fig. S9). The first principle density functional theory (DFT) calculations were employed to further verify the effect of the precursor on the RTP performance of CDs. Based on the structure of the precursor, the results show that the energy gap of the four precursors is also gradually decreasing (0.21354, 0.18162, 0.15004, and 0.13543 eV), which are consistent with the trends of the energy gaps changes for N-CDs, and verify that the four precursors have an increasing degree of conjugation. An increase in the molecular conjugation size typically leads to enhanced electron delocalization within the conjugated system, thereby reducing the HOMO-LUMO energy gap [51]. This facilitates the transition of electrons from HOMO to LUMO, and consequently, the energy gap decreases with the increase in molecular conjugation size. This phenomenon plays a crucial role in the redshift of the emission wavelength. Furthermore, the trend of the energy gap variation is consistent with the trend of the phosphorescence lifetime changes at the four N-CDs. N-CDs-I exhibits the longest phosphorescence lifetime, whereas N-CDs-IV displays the shortest. This observation is attributed to the longer phosphorescence emission wavelength of N-CDs-IV, which corresponds to a lower energy level of the T1 state. A lower T1 energy level augments the energy gap between the singlet S1 and the triplet T1 states, thereby making it more difficult for excitons in the S1 state to undergo intersystem crossing (ISC) to reach the T1 state, which is detrimental to phosphorescence emission [52]. In contrast, N-CDs-I, with its shorter wavelength and higher energy, slows down non-radiative transitions, resulting in an extended phosphorescence lifetime. We conjecture that where the degree of conjugation of aromatic compounds plays a crucial role in modulating the afterglow performance system. Organic small molecules with different conjugated structures possess distinct energy gaps. By interacting with the matrix, they form carbon dot-based composite materials with different emission centers, which in turn affect the band structure and optical properties of the carbon dots, thereby achieving different persistent afterglow emissions. At the same time, AB matrix plays an important role in the phosphorescent emission, AB provides a rigid structure that protects the CD molecules' triplet excitons, while the CDs can covalently bond with borate ions through BC and NHCO bonds, while ammonium ions can form hydrogen bond interactions with functional groups on the surface of CD, realizing phosphorescent under the action of multi-confined. (Fig. 4f).

To explore the universality of molecular regulation in afterglow phenomena, a comprehensive series of experiments was carried out. Firstly, a non-aromatic cyclic compound (citric acid), a typical conjugated acid, was employed as a precursor, and N-CDs-CA was prepared by the same treatment method. The phosphorescence emission spectrum (Fig. S10) shows that its emission is concentrated in the green region, indicating that the AB is applicable for activating the phosphorescence of molecular substances, albeit with a lower luminescence intensity compared to aromatic compounds. To substantiate that aromatic compounds as precursors can elicit superior afterglow performance within this matrix and possess significant universality in color regulation, we also selected molecules with different degrees of conjugation and similar structures (phthalic acid, biphenyl dicarboxylic acid, homophthalic tetracarboxylic acid, 1,8-naphthalenedicarboxylic anhydride and 1.4.5.8-naphthalenetetracarboxylic acid) and prepared various different CDs (N-CDs-PA, N-CDs-DA, N-CDs-OA, N-CDs-NA, N-CDs-SA) in the same manner. Consistent with our expectations, with an increase in molecular conjugation, the emission peak shifted towards the red (longer wavelength) region (Fig. 4g, Figs. S11-15). This redshift indicates a lower energy of the emitted light, which corresponds to longer wavelengths in the spectrum. This is usually associated with an increased delocalization of π −electrons on larger conjugated systems, which leads to a stabilization of the excited states and lower energy transitions. Additionally, the redshift in the UV absorption spectrum also verified our hypothesis (Fig. S16), the increasing rigidity of the molecular structure, achieved by the extension of the conjugated system, may lead to a decrease in the energy required for the electronic transition, resulting in a redshift. The detail optical characteristics of the N-CDs-based RTP materials prepared from different precursor molecules are listed in Table S3. In summary, it is both straightforward and practical to modulate the afterglow of CDs by manipulating the molecular conjugate structure, providing a universal direction for preparing CD based RTP materials with tunable-color in the visible light region.

During the phosphorescence process, after a molecule absorbs radiation and transitions from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (S1), it does not directly fall back to S0. Instead, it undergoes intersystem crossing to reach the excited triplet state (T1), and then returns to the ground state S0 via a radiative transition. In contrast, thermally activated delayed fluorescence involves an additional reverse intersystem crossing before radiatively returning to the ground state. Compared to fluorescence, the T1 state in phosphorescence cannot be directly populated from S0 by photon absorption; it must go through the S–T transition, which is a forbidden process. Additionally, the triplet state is sensitive to factors such as water, oxygen, and temperature, leading to non-radiative decay of phosphorescence.

Based on the above results, the phosphorescence mechanism of N-CDs can be summarized. At higher temperatures, N-CDs are generated in situ and effectively embedded within a rigid structure of AB. First, the crystal structure of AB consists of a borate anion and an ammonium cation. Within the lattice, the boron and oxygen atoms form a polyhedral structure, and the ammonium cation forms an ionic bond with the borate anion. As the reaction proceeds, the rigid structure of AB provides a strong multi-confinement effect, boron-oxygen polyhedron forms a BC covalent bond with the CDs surface, the free ammonia also form hydrogen bonds with surface functional groups of CDs, which limit the vibration and rotation of CD molecules together, stabilize the triplet excitons, inhibiting non-radiative decay and thus achieve efficient phosphorescence emission [20,53–56]. Moreover, the degree of conjugation of the precursors affects the conjugation size of the carbon core [57], which can be evidenced by the increase in CD size in TEM images and the red shift of the UV absorption peak. This increased conjugation leads to a smaller energy gap between T1 and S0, resulting in a red shift of the afterglow emission wavelength. Eventually, the phosphorescent emission from blue to red was achieved (Fig. 4h). Additionally, the presence of graphitic nitrogen in CDs is conducive to n-π* transitions and enhances the efficiency of intersystem crossing, significantly promoting the redshift of the emission wavelength. Furthermore, all four precursor molecules contain benzene or naphthalene rings, as well as conjugated double bond systems. Their molecular structures tend to be planar, which facilitates the delocalization of electrons [58]. The conjugation effect increases the degree of conjugation of π electrons, reduces the internal conversion and intersystem crossing processes, as well as the energy loss of non-radiative transitions such as intramolecular vibrations, thereby enhancing the phosphorescence efficiency [59]. The multiple confinement effects between AB and CDs, combined with the rigid planar structure of the precursor molecules, stabilize the excited state, reduce non-radiative relaxation, thus achieving ultrahigh quantum efficiency [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CDs based RTP materials
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely recognized that environmental water and oxygen molecules significantly influence phosphorescence performance. We observed that the long afterglow phenomenon disappeared and almost no phosphorescence emission signal was detected when the sample was prepared as an aqueous solution or in contact with water. This is attributed to the instability of triplet excitons, which are readily quenched by water and oxygen in the environment. To substantiate that water indeed impacts the RTP emission of N-CDs, we measured the phosphorescence spectrum of the powder after alternating exposure to water and heat. Strikingly, when the powder was heated on a hot plate at 180 °C for 30 min, it returned to a dry state, and phosphorescence reemerged, thus confirming the influence of water on the RTP properties. The cycle of heating and dissolution was repeated five times, and it was observed that the phosphorescence signal essentially disappeared after each dissolution, but could be restored after subsequent heating. This indicates that the four types of N-CDs powders exhibit cyclic reversibility in their RTP response to water/heat stimulation (Fig. S17). In conclusion, the presence of water disrupts hydrogen bonds, which can be reestablished by removing water through the process of heating.

Owing to their distinctive color-tunable RTP characteristics and cyclic reversibility, N-CDs have demonstrated immense potential in various fields, such as anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, N-CDs-I powder was filled into a simple plum blossom shape. When the 254 nm UV light is turned off, it exhibits a deep blue color, and when the 365 nm UV light is turned off, it shows a cyan-blue color. Considering the phosphorescent properties of different colors, a simple shamrock pattern was created for anti-counterfeiting purposes. The upper leaf, lateral leaves, and basal stem were filled with N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, and N-CDs-III powders, respectively. After removing the 254 nm UV light, the upper leaf assumes a deep blue appearance, the lateral leaves display a dark yellow tint, and the basal stem adopts a dark green coloration. For the pattern excited by the 365 nm UV light, the upper leaf showcases a cyan-blue shade, the lateral leaves manifest a bright yellow color, and the basal stem exhibits a bright green hue. Therefore, this unique light-stimulated RTP phenomenon is difficult to imitate, proving the potential of these materials in anti-counterfeiting pattern applications. The N-CDs were also used in applications similar to Morse code for encrypting and decrypting information. In this system, the phosphorescent color of N-CDs-II was assigned “0″ in Morse code, while that of N-CDs-III was assigned as ”1″. By strategically arranging these colors, Morse code groups such as “111″, ”0101″, “10″, ”110″ were generated, which can be decrypted into “SCAU” (Fig. 5b). In addition to the aforementioned applications, we have also designed multi-level encoding using time-resolved dynamic color conversion, forming dynamic 2D codes into 5D codes. As shown in the Fig. 5c, the matrix pattern of phosphorescent emission under various excitation light sources and at different time intervals constitutes an independent 3D code, each imbued with distinct information. Under the daylight lamp, N-CDs-I, N-CDs-II, N-CDs-III, and N-CDs-IV exhibit white, yellow, light pink, and light orange, respectively. After removing the WLED excitation light source, a 3D Code 1 matrix pattern was formed. The pattern that constitutes Code 2 was the matrix pattern that appears after the deactivation of 365 nm UV light, which was considered false information. After this false information disappeared, it transitioned to the next level of information A, i.e., Code 3, with a time delay of 0.1 s. The information then passed to the next level of information B, i.e., Code 4, with a 1-second delay. After changing the excitation wavelength to 254 nm, a new 3D code (Code 5) appeared, displaying information C. Thus, a series of tunable codes serving as information carriers were obtained at different time points and under different excitation wavelengths, demonstrating the great potential of full-color N-CDs in advanced information storage and data encryption. Based on the remarkable afterglow emission capabilities of the full-color N-CDs RTP materials, a trait that underscores its immense potential for a wide array of applications, particularly in the realms of information anti-counterfeiting and encryption, but also stands as a testament to the innovative strides being made in enhancing data security and authenticity verification.

In summary, we have successfully developed an innovative strategy that combines AB with a variety of organic molecules through a one-step pyrolysis process to prepare CD-based afterglow materials with outstanding performance. Notably, the N-CDs feature an ultra-high quantum yield (QY) of 81.13 % and a long afterglow lifetime of 880 ms, with an afterglow duration exceeding 8 s, making it one of the best-performing metal-free RTP materials to date. This unique phosphorescent property is attributed to the multiple confinement effects, BC covalent bonds and hydrogen bond interactions that effectively suppress the non-radiative deactivation of the triplet excited state in CD clusters. More impressively, by choosing the different structures of the precursors, the phosphorescence color and lifetime of the N-CDs can be effectively modulated, resulting in more efficient, ultra-long lifetime of CDs based on RTP materials. Owing to their superior properties, these composite materials hold great potential for applications in the advanced fields of information security and anti-counterfeiting. This strategy also points towards a promising direction for the development of efficient phosphorescent materials with tunable colors, in order to explore their broader application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.159246.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
